author: @stevecummins324
text:
instead of a single 34 foot water column. split it into smaller columns. a tube full of air can be treated as having same pressure throughout it... so a gas filled tube can transfer a pressure drop to a lower elevation..
eg...  
first bottom tank. pressure on  free water surface bottom tank is atmospheric (aprox 1bar absolute pressure). tube with water column going up say 2m. pressure drop at top of water -rho*g*h=1000*10*2= -20kPa or -0.2bar

vacuum hose to transfer that pressure drop in gas, to gas to above water level in next bottom tank. it's f water surface will start at atmospheric - 0.2bar ie 0.8bar absolute. and a 2m high water column takes another 0.2bar from pressure... resulting in -0.4bar from atmospheric. ie 0.6bar absolute....

repeat until pressure is reduced sufficiently.

author: @jaishetty8586
text:
I suppose I am a bit late here, but have any of you tried the wave power to pressurise water to enable reverse Osmosis ? I have been studying this concept for a while now. The lashing waves could lift a buoyant float and let it fall due to gravity, and this movement could be converted in to a pumping action to operate the reverse Osmosis pumps. 
Alternatively, we could also use this wave power to develop some great Vacuum and then reduce the column height to enable flash boiling at ambient temperature. I understand, the goal is to not involve any electronics, and seals..must be simple and stupid, yet reliable like an axe. 

Let me know your thoughts. 

About pulling vacuum from centrifugal force of spinning water, isn't it as good as using a liquid ring pump ?

author: @fishyerik
text:
So found this trying to google why the basic principle of low temperature water column vacuum distillation is not already, since "forever", widely applied. So, I think it is time for you to revisit this subject, I want to know if you have gotten any further.  :) 
 I want to point out that neither the evaporator or condenser has to be incorporated in water columns, you can design the evaporator and the condenser for optimal respective main function, and have a separate vacuum pump in the form of a water column in a thin hose. 
You can then just keep the evaporator in the sun, and the condenser shadowed from the sun basically, or use basically any source of waste heat that is not insignificant. 
10+ meter plus buildings, or trees for that matter, are not that rare phenomena. I think having the water column vacuum "pump" as a separate part of the system brings huge advantages. Because you want as much evaporation and condensation as possible, and as little complexity as possible.  
Spraying, mechanical pumps, and so on may very well increase output from a specific system, or even be needed for some designs to work at all,  but it adds unnecessary complexity if you do not actually need it. And when dealing with brine is one of those examples when you should be extra careful to avoid unnecessary mechanical complexity.
You almost seemed to forget that evaporating and condensing large amounts of water means large amounts of energy transferred/transformed. Both the evaporator and the condenser needs effective heat transfer. It think huge surface area is needed in order to get relevant amounts of distilled water if only the difference between sun exposure and ambient air in the shadow is utilized. I do not think the surface area needed makes the principle unviable for practical use, far from it, surface area is not that complicated to supply, but I think it is easy to vastly underestimate the area needed. 
Also, I don't think it is a good idea to completely separate salt and water in the system. The higher the salinity the lower the output will get, all else equal, and with "moderate" temperature differences/energy transfer used, at saturation it would be a lot more difficult to get significant output compared to, say, at 6-7% salt content. Sure, roughly twice the amount of seawater would be needed, transport of it, and so on, but, that is not the tricky part of desalinization in most cases. Humans learned to transport water fairly efficiently thousands of years ago. Also, harvesting salt from within the system presents another plethora of possible issues. Replacing still far from saturated brine with sea water in the system is not that problematic.      
Also, about the water column height needed to reduce pressure enough, one of the interesting things with water columns is that they do not need to consist of one single straight column for physics to work as it does. If height is really an issue, you can have multiple water columns work in series, connected in series, and mounted side by side.

author: @johns.5864
text:
An alternative to using fixed width columns might to to have the lower portion of the column being a 35 foot vertical pipe below ground and the upper portion being a tank several feet in height and diameter above ground.  The hole for the below ground pipe would be drilled in the same manner as a water well is drilled.

For the two column implementation one tank provides the large volume for evaporation and the other tank provides the large volume for condensation.  The tall underground pipes provide the height required to produce the vacuums for each without using unnecessary volume.

In short the column is wide above ground but tall and narrow below ground providing the benefits of both width and height only where needed.

author: @largefarmant6798
text:
I have an idea. You have two columns but you only need one with a partition in the middle. Fresh sea water comes up one side and the heavier brine goes down the other. As one commentator said a Tesla fluidic one way valve could keep the flow going the right direction. At the top you have a partition where fresh water collects. It is above the sea water and brine. At the bottom of this partition you have a hole that is plugged. The plug pushes in from the outside. A float controls the valve. When enough water builds up 1. float goes up 2. float pressure pushes the valve out letting the a little water (and maybe some gas pressure) out.

Now I have a string connected to the wall of the fresh water collection partition, then to the plug, then to the float. The float going up would pull the string, pushing the plug out. Probably best to use a lever but the sketch tool I used was hell with a trackball so I did the best I could.

It would not be high volume unless the column was large but it would be steady and require no pumps or other equipment. I don't see why you couldn't just sink it in the ocean leaving the top sticking out ten feet or so so the sun would hit it.

Volume of fresh water would increase the more you shaded the fresh water collection and the more you heated the salt water intake side.

Here's a sketch online. Excuse my pitiful sketching. Track balls as not made for drawing.

http://www.sketchtoy.com/66588334

I might add the centrifuge idea is brilliant as is your idea in the first place.


Here's another idea that's a bit flaky. There's a pump called a spiral pump. It works by compressing air in a spiral. 1/2 water 1/2 air. The water pressures add per every spiral. A very nifty thing. Could you fill a spiral pump with water. Seal it and lift out of the water and get your vacuum from the spirals? Maybe have one spiral for salt water in and one out with brine? A fresh water take off at the top. I can't get my head around the idea of whether they would add or not? They do so as a pump but it does have a air buffer. Anyways here's a link to spiral pumps. Truly interesting in themselves.

http://lurkertech.com/water/pump/tailer/

author: @sasjadv
text:
I just had a bit of an eureka moment, this is how you can put the whole thing in your living room:
So there is a vacuum in the tube because of gravity; and if the gravity would be stronger, the tube can be shorter.
The solution: Centrifugal force!!!
...
I did a quick calculation: if you want the device to be 1×1×1meter in stead of 10 meters, you can make a spinning can with the diameter of 1 meter (so radius=50cm) the centrifugal acceleration should be at least 20 times bigger than our gravity(9,81m/s²)
A=V²÷r   =>   20×9,81m/s²=V²÷0,50meter   => V²=0,50×20×9,81=98,1    So V=squareroot(98,1)=9,9 Meters/second.
10m/s for a diameter of 1meter gives 10÷2п=1,59turns per seconds =95 RPM, this is really the minimum for it to 'fit' in your living room.
Only think about the majority of the washing machines reaching 1000rpm, this must be easy to realise

Just add filtered dirty water and electricity.

author: @joeglory425
text:
Another practical consideration is moisture carryover which would bring along with it salt contamination. In high pressure/high temperature boiling such as in a power plant where the water boils at 500 F and 1000 psig, the steam quantity is only around 10% with 90% liquid water being carried along by the steam. Remember steam is invisible. When you "see" a steam plume coming off your tea kettle you're actually seeing the liquid water that is mixed with the steam. In a power plant they send the steam/water mixture first through a moisture separator and then a steam drier. I think a low tech solution would be to pass the steam/water mixture through some cotton or maybe even hay or straw on it's way to the condenser.

What I don't know is whether carryover is nearly the same problem with low pressure boiling as it is with high pressure boiling. I suppose one could just test the salinity of the condensate and if it's unacceptable then add in the moisture separator.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's going to be a question of testing that mate - but i don't think that will be a serious problem given the nature of the system - still testing will tell
